If you have these available, feel free to fax along with this form. - Patient demographic information (including insurance info) - Recent H&P or recent progress notes - Medication List THANK YOU FOR YOUR REFERRAL!!! From: To:17023428398 04/09/2026 12:14 #138 P.002/005 Home Health Protocol Dr. Khavkin uses two different techniques to close the wound, depending on the patient's skin and overall body type. 1. The most common type of closure involves subcutaneous absorbable sutures with the skin closed with the glue (Dermabond) and a Steri-strip that is attached to the incision. On top of this, he puts a piece of Telfa dressing covered with a tape. The tape and Telfa can come off two days after the surgery. The glue and the Steri-Strips are waterproof and it's ok for the patient to take shower but not a bath or pools. The Steri-Strips usually fall off about two weeks after the surgery or Dr. Khavkin removes it when he sees the patient for a follow-up appointment 2 weeks after the surgery. Do not remove Stril Strip by yourself. 2. The second type of closure is when he uses staples, which then are covered with the antibiotic ointment and the Telfa dressing with the tape on top of it. With this kind of closure, the patient should not be taking shower without the incision being covered till the follow up appointment. The dressing needs to be replaced by Home Health Care team during each visit. At any point, if the HHC team member sees any concerns with the wound healing such as redness, swelling, opening of the wound or any kind of discharge, Dr.Khavkin and his staff need to be notified immediately. You should also notify Dr. Khavkin's staff if the patient has persistent pain, difficulty swallowing, signs of infection or any numbness, tingling or weakness in the extremities. You can start gentle physical therapy on the extremities without working on the area of the surgery (neck or back). Dr. Khavkin usually starts formal outpatient physical therapy around 4-6 weeks after the surgery.
